title: Untitled
research_problem: Developing a probabilistic model to predict pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections for connected vehicles and autonomous driving safety systems. The specific problem is estimating pedestrian decisions (cross or wait) and motion types (standing, walking, running) to prevent collisions between turning vehicles and pedestrians.
domain: Intelligent Transportation Systems, Autonomous Vehicles, Pedestrian Safety
method_summary: A Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) model that probabilistically integrates relationships among traffic signal context (pedestrian green, flashing green, red phases), pedestrian decisions (cross/wait), motion types (standing/walking/running), and physical movement (position, speed, direction). The model uses particle filtering for state estimation including position, crossing decision, and motion type.
method_family: Probabilistic Graphical Models (Dynamic Bayesian Network) + Particle Filter
datasets: Real traffic data collected from a vehicle at a signalized intersection in Japan using three monocular cameras. Contains 289 pedestrian sequences: 251 cross-pedestrians and 38 wait-pedestrians. PG duration: 35s, PFG duration: 10s.
tasks: Pedestrian crossing decision recognition (cross vs wait)
Pedestrian motion type classification (standing, walking, running)
metrics: Positioning error (mean and standard deviation in meters)
baselines: Previous model by Iryo-Asano et al. (2015) - uses logistic regression with distance to crosswalk and traveling speed at onset of PFG as explanatory variables

main_claims.claim_text: The proposed DBN+PF system correctly recognizes pedestrian crossing decisions with >85% accuracy after 2 seconds of observation across different noise levels (rn = 0.1, 0.4, 1.0 m)
The system recognizes wait decisions with 85% accuracy after 3-4 seconds of observation, taking longer than cross decisions due to deceleration cues
The proposed probabilistic model provides confidence scores (probabilities) for states rather than binary classifications, enabling varied vehicle behaviors based on confidence levels
The DBN model successfully integrates contextual information (traffic signal phases) with pedestrian physical states, mimicking human decision-making processes
Walking motion recognition maintains 90% accuracy across noise levels, while running motion recognition is more challenging due to overlapping speed distributions with walking

limitations.text: The model assumes straight-line pedestrian trajectories, but the dataset contains curved trajectories that cause positioning errors in cross-scenarios
High observation noise causes recognition delays - larger errors make it difficult to estimate correct pedestrian speed for distinguishing decisions
Running motion is difficult to distinguish from walking due to overlapping speed distributions - speed alone is insufficient as a classification feature
The model assumes pedestrians make crossing/wait decisions at onset of PFG, but pedestrians may change decisions later based on traffic conditions (e.g., turning vehicles)
Highly imbalanced dataset with only 38 wait-pedestrians vs 251 cross-pedestrians due to data collection constraints
Computational efficiency is limited - algorithm spends 5.1s to process 1.0s of data (13 frames) on a single-threaded implementation

future_work: Incorporate additional contextual factors such as existence and positions of turning vehicles into the model
Develop path prediction for curved trajectories by learning turning motion as a spatial feature
Integrate visual features from images (temporal features) to improve running vs walking classification
Add time elapsed from PFG onset as an explanatory variable for pedestrians entering after PFG starts
Implement multi-threaded/parallel processing to improve computational efficiency
notes: The paper focuses on near-side pedestrians at left-turning scenarios (left-hand traffic in Japan). Traffic signal has three phases: PG (pedestrian green), PFG (pedestrian flashing green), PR (pedestrian red). Pedestrians are allowed to cross during PG, cannot start crossing during PR, and PFG is critical for decision-making. The model uses logistic regression for decision modeling and gamma distributions for speed modeling. Particle filter with 2000 particles is used for state estimation.
